For restoration accounting, Ethiopia assumes natural forest restoration reaches carbon equilibrium within 20 years and averages total biomass gain over that period; commercial forest planting uses mean annual volume increment from five plantation species plus IPCC conversion factors and root-to-shoot ratios.
For natural forest restoration, it is conservatively assumed that all carbon pools in tropical systems reach equilibrium within 20 years. Accordingly, the total biomass gain... resulting from the conversion of non-forest land to forestland through assisted natural regeneration is averaged over this 20-year period... For commercial forest planting, a different approach was adopted due to limited biome-specific inventory data. The mean annual volume increment of Ethiopia’s five major plantation species... was applied... converted to aboveground biomass growth using IPCC biomass conversion and expansion factors (BCEFs)... while belowground biomass was estimated using standard root-to-shoot ratios.

text:
Local people in Sewne reportedly prohibit removal of live plants and the sale of fuelwood or charcoal, while allowing dead wood collection, grazing, and beekeeping.
Nobody may cut live plants for fuel or construction. Dead and dried wood may be collected, but no one may generate income though selling fuel or charcoal. Animals are allowed to graze in the forest; beekeeping is likewise allowed.

The paper describes the traditional daget technique as an untilled grass strip at the lower plot boundary that reduces runoff, increases infiltration, and traps sediment.

text:
The dissertation's study area is Bururi Catchment in western Ethiopia, and the work focuses on land use/cover dynamics and their impacts on erosion rate, soil quality, soil organic carbon, and total nitrogen stock.
"LAND USE/COVER DYNAMICS AND ITS IMPACTS ON EROSION RATE, SOIL QUALITY, AND SOIL ORGANIC CARBON AND TOTAL NITROGEN STOCK AT BURURI CATCHMENT, WESTERN ETHIOPIA"

text:
The study found that mean annual soil loss in Bururi Catchment increased from 41.04 t ha−1 y−1 in 1957 to 48.91 t ha−1 y−1 in 2018, indicating worsening erosion over time.
"mean annual soil loss increased from 41.04 t ha-1 y-1 in 1957 to 48.91 t ha-1 y-1 in 2018."

text:
LULC conversions substantially reduced several soil quality parameters; forest cover and shrub land converted to agricultural and degraded land depleted organic matter, soil pH, CEC, and increased bulk density.
"The conversions of native ecosystem into managed land use system substantially reduced the studied soil quality parameters. Particularly, the change of forest cover and shrub land into agricultural and degraded land caused a depletion of sand content, soil pH, organic matter, and CEC and increase bulk density, which is associated to soil compaction."

text:
The study used a GIS-based RUSLE model with station rainfall data, soil texture and organic carbon content, a 30 m DEM, and LU/LC maps from 1957, 1987, and 2018 to estimate erosion risk.
"The soil loss of the study catchment was computed by employing a GIS-based Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE) model. To generate the required inputs for the model, station-based rainfall data; soil texture and organic carbon content; 30m by 30m digital elevation model (DEM); and LU/LC Maps of three periods (1957, 1987, and 2018) have been employed."

text:
The study sampled thirty soil samples from five dominant LULC types and two elevation categories, and identified eight soil quality parameters via PCA.
"thirty soil samples (0-20cm depth) collected from five dominant LULC types and two elevation categories... only eight parameters (such as sand content, bulk density, soil pH, organic matter, total nitrogen, available phosphorus, CEC, and carbon to nitrogen ratio) were identified as appropriate soil quality parameters by using principal component analysis (PCA)."

text:
The dissertation states that forest cover and cultivated lands experienced lower erosion rates, while shrubs, grass, and bare lands experienced higher erosion rates due to overgrazing and vegetation removal.
"Forest cover and cultivated lands experienced low rate, while shrubs, grass, and bare lands have increased rate of erosion... the higher rate of erosion on shrubs, grass, and bare lands is likely caused by overgrazing and removal of vegetation cover from those land cover types."

text:
The study reports that erosion in Bururi Catchment remained above accepted tolerance thresholds, including the 5 to 11 t ha−1 y−1 tolerance range and the national maximum tolerable loss estimate of 18 t ha−1 y−1.
"This mean value is still greater than the normal soil loss tolerance value (5 to 11 t ha-1 y-1) suggested by Renard et al. (1996) and the national maximum tolerable soil loss estimate (18 t ha-1 y-1) recommended by Hurni (1985)."

"appropriate soil conservation measures should be prioritized in the catchment... Conservation planners have to design proper soil and water conservation activities and implement based on the severity levels of soil erosion in the catchment."
